past_medical_history: Polycystic ovary syndrome diagnosed at age 22
Mild obstructive sleep apnea, non-compliant with CPAP
Anxiety disorder treated with sertraline for the past 3 years
No history of febrile seizures or central nervous system infections

medications.indication: Insulin resistance associated with polycystic ovary syndrome

family_history: Father with type 2 diabetes and coronary artery disease
One sibling (brother) with a history of a single unprovoked seizure at age 28, never treated
No family history of epilepsy syndromes or neurodegenerative conditions

social_history.alcohol: Occasional glass of wine on weekends, approximately 2-3 per month
social_history.recreational_drugs: Denies any current or prior illicit drug use
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband and two children (ages 8 and 12) in suburban home
social_history.driving: Drives daily for work commute and children's activities
social_history.exercise: Walks 3-4 times per week, occasional yoga class

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Mildly anxious in demeanor. Speech fluent with normal rate and prosody. No paraphasic errors. Naming and repetition intact. Follows complex three-step commands. Registers 3/3 objects; recalls 2/3 at five minutes without cues, 3/3 with categorical cues. Able to spell WORLD backwards correctly. Draws intersecting pentagons accurately.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light and accommodation bilaterally (3.5 mm to 2 mm). Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact to light touch and pinprick bilaterally. Facial movements symmetric at rest and with activation. Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Gag reflex present bilaterally. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline, no fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.motor: Normal bulk and tone in all four extremities. No spasticity or rigidity. Strength 5/5 in all major muscle groups bilaterally (deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, finger abductors, hip flexors, quadriceps, hamstrings, tibialis anterior, gastrocnemius). No pronator drift. No tremor or involuntary movements.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, temperature, vibration, and proprioception intact throughout. No extinction to double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin testing normal bilaterally without dysmetria or intention tremor. Rapid alternating movements intact and symmetric.
neurological_exam.gait: Gait normal with symmetric arm swing. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No evidence of tongue bite or other oral trauma at time of examination. Husband reports that during one witnessed episode approximately two weeks ago the patient appeared to chew repeatedly and pick at her clothing for about one minute, after which she appeared confused for several minutes.

patient.chief_complaint: Recurrent episodes of staring and unresponsiveness, sometimes with repetitive hand and mouth movements, occurring approximately twice monthly for the past eight months
patient.history_present_illness:
Mrs. Kapoor is a 41-year-old right-handed female high school chemistry teacher who presents for inpatient evaluation of recurrent episodic spells of altered awareness that have been occurring approximately twice monthly over the past eight months. She was referred by her primary care physician after her husband witnessed two distinct episodes and became increasingly concerned.

The patient herself has limited recall of the events. She reports that on several occasions at work, colleagues have told her she appeared to suddenly stop mid-sentence while lecturing, stared blankly for what seemed like a minute, and then seemed confused for several minutes afterward. She did not fall during any of these episodes and had no preceding warning that she could recall. Her husband has witnessed two episodes at home. During the most recent event two weeks ago, he describes her suddenly becoming unresponsive while they were watching television. She appeared to make repetitive chewing motions with her mouth and was picking at her blouse with her right hand. He called her name repeatedly but she did not respond. The episode lasted approximately 60 to 90 seconds, after which she appeared disoriented and asked him what had happened. She seemed confused for about five minutes before returning to her baseline. He noted that her speech seemed slightly slurred during the recovery period.

When specifically asked, the patient is uncertain whether she experiences any preceding sensations, though she notes that on a few occasions she has had a brief feeling of something being oddly familiar, which she attributed to distraction or daydreaming. She has never had a generalized convulsion, tongue bite, or urinary incontinence during any episode. She denies any preceding headaches, visual disturbances, lightheadedness, or palpitations. She reports feeling more fatigued recently and has had some difficulty concentrating at work, which she attributes to poor sleep related to her untreated sleep apnea and work-related stress. There has been no recent weight change, fever, or other systemic complaints. Her anxiety has been stable on sertraline.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Stage II sleep captured with vertex waves and sleep spindles. Sleep architecture appears mildly fragmented.

findings.frequency: Rare, approximately 3-4 noted during the 45-minute recording
findings.morphology: Sharply contoured waveforms with phase reversal at F8, duration 50-80 ms, followed by a subtle after-going slow wave. Morphology not definitively epileptiform versus benign sharp transients.
findings.state: Two noted during drowsiness, one during wakefulness, one during light sleep
findings.clinical_correlation: Suspicious for right anterior temporal epileptiform activity but morphology is equivocal. Clinical correlation and prolonged monitoring recommended.

findings.frequency: Intermittent bursts of theta-range slowing, 4-6 Hz, lasting 2-5 seconds, noted on multiple occasions
findings.morphology: Polymorphic theta activity, slightly more prominent than the left temporal region
findings.clinical_correlation: Right temporal intermittent slowing may suggest underlying structural or functional abnormality in the right temporal region.

artifacts.impact: Moderate, intermittently obscuring temporal discharges during wakefulness. Less prominent during drowsiness when sharps were better visualized.
Expected, no impact on temporal chain interpretation

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Three minutes of hyperventilation performed. Generalized background slowing noted, symmetric. No clear focal abnormality provoked.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response at 10-18 Hz bilaterally. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Mildly abnormal EEG. Rare sharply contoured transients noted in the right anterior temporal region (F8/T4) with equivocal epileptiform morphology. Intermittent right temporal polymorphic theta slowing also noted. These findings are suspicious for, but not definitively diagnostic of, a right temporal epileptogenic focus. No electrographic seizures captured during this routine recording. Prolonged video-EEG monitoring is strongly recommended to better characterize these transients and to capture a habitual clinical event.
eeg.limitations: Routine 45-minute recording with no clinical events captured. Temporal muscle artifact intermittently limited assessment. Recording duration insufficient to confidently classify rare sharp transients.
recommended_actions: Prolonged video-EEG monitoring (minimum 72 hours) to capture habitual events and further characterize interictal abnormalities
MRI brain with 3T epilepsy protocol including thin coronal cuts through temporal lobes
Clinical correlation with detailed semiology from witnessed events

findings.size: Mild volumetric asymmetry; right hippocampus appears slightly smaller than left on visual inspection of coronal T2 and FLAIR sequences

signal_characteristics.T1: Borderline loss of normal internal architecture of the right hippocampus compared to the left, most apparent on thin coronal sections
signal_characteristics.T2: Questionable mild hyperintensity in the right hippocampal body compared to the left; could represent early sclerosis versus normal variant
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Equivocal subtle signal increase in the right hippocampal body; not clearly abnormal in isolation

findings.borders: No discrete mass or focal cortical dysplasia identified

signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense punctate foci in bilateral periventricular white matter

volumetrics.right_hippocampus: 2.8 cm3 (10th percentile for age and sex)
volumetrics.left_hippocampus: 3.3 cm3 (40th percentile for age and sex)

additional_observations: No evidence of focal cortical dysplasia or heterotopia
No mass lesion or pathological enhancement after gadolinium
Ventricular system normal in size and configuration
Periventricular white matter foci are nonspecific and may represent small vessel changes or migrainous changes given patient age
No evidence of prior hemorrhage or infarction
mri.impression: Subtle right hippocampal asymmetry with borderline volume reduction and equivocal FLAIR signal abnormality. The hippocampal volumetric asymmetry index of 0.08 falls below the definitive threshold for abnormal (0.10), though the right hippocampus is at the 10th percentile for age and sex. A few nonspecific periventricular white matter punctate foci are noted, likely related to migraine or small vessel changes. No mass lesion, acute infarction, or obvious structural epileptogenic lesion identified. Clinical and electrophysiological correlation recommended.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Early or subtle right mesial temporal sclerosis
differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Possible but not definitive on current imaging

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Normal hippocampal variant with mild asymmetry

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Possible if clinical and EEG data support temporal epilepsy

recommended_actions: Quantitative hippocampal volumetry with automated segmentation for more precise comparison
Consider repeat imaging with 3T epilepsy protocol if not yet performed
Consider FDG-PET for additional lateralizing data

ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm with no acute abnormalities. No arrhythmia or conduction disorder identified. ECG does not suggest a cardiac etiology for the patient's episodic altered awareness.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Normal ECG reduces the likelihood of cardiac arrhythmia as a cause of episodic loss of awareness.

BMP.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated fasting glucose, consistent with insulin resistance in the context of polycystic ovary syndrome. Not a cause of the episodic events.

Additional.clinical_significance: Prolactin within normal range. However, blood was drawn approximately 3 hours after last witnessed episode, well beyond the 20-minute window for reliable post-ictal prolactin elevation.
Vitamin D insufficiency. Not related to the patient's presenting episodes but should be addressed.
Negative urine drug screen excludes drug-provoked seizure

labs.interpretation: Complete blood count and basic metabolic panel are essentially normal. Fasting glucose is mildly elevated at 104 mg/dL, consistent with known insulin resistance. Prolactin is within normal limits but was drawn well outside the optimal post-ictal window. TSH is normal. Vitamin D is low at 22 ng/mL. Urine drug screen is negative. No metabolic derangement identified that would explain the patient's episodic altered awareness.
abnormal_values_summary: Glucose 104 mg/dL (mildly elevated, reference 70-100 mg/dL) - consistent with insulin resistance, not related to episodes
Vitamin D 22 ng/mL (low, reference 30-100 ng/mL) - insufficiency, unrelated to presenting complaint

background.sleep_features: Sleep architecture captured over 96 hours. Mildly fragmented sleep with frequent arousals, consistent with known obstructive sleep apnea.

findings.frequency: Occasional, averaging 5-6 per hour during NREM sleep, 1-2 per hour during wakefulness
findings.morphology: Phase reversal at F8 with field extending to T4 and occasionally to F4. Duration 40-70 ms with consistent after-going slow wave. Morphology now clearly epileptiform on prolonged recording with multiple examples for comparison.
findings.state: All states, maximal during NREM sleep stage II
findings.clinical_correlation: Confirms a right anterior temporal epileptogenic focus

findings.location: Right anterior temporal onset (F8/T4), evolving to right hemispheric then bilateral
findings.frequency: One clinical seizure captured on day 3 of monitoring
findings.morphology: Seizure onset with right anterior temporal low-amplitude fast activity (beta-range), evolving to 5-6 Hz rhythmic theta over right temporal chain within 10 seconds, then progressive slowing to 3-4 Hz rhythmic delta with spread to right parasagittal leads by 35 seconds. Total electrographic duration 78 seconds. No bilateral tonic-clonic evolution.
findings.state: Occurred during relaxed wakefulness while patient was watching television
findings.clinical_correlation: Clinical correlate: patient suddenly stopped responding to conversation, stared ahead with dilated pupils, developed repetitive lip-smacking and right hand picking movements at clothing. Husband at bedside confirmed this was identical to her habitual episodes. Post-ictally, patient was confused and could not name objects shown to her for approximately 3 minutes, with full recovery over 7 minutes.

findings.frequency: Intermittent runs of polymorphic theta-delta slowing
findings.clinical_correlation: Persistent right temporal slowing supports underlying structural or functional right temporal abnormality

artifacts.location: Bilateral temporal chains, intermittent during wakefulness
artifacts.impact: Moderate during wakefulness, minimal during sleep when interictal activity best appreciated

output.impression: Abnormal 96-hour continuous video-EEG monitoring. One habitual clinical seizure captured with clear right anterior temporal electrographic onset (F8/T4) concordant with clinical semiology of temporal lobe seizure (behavioral arrest, oral automatisms, right hand automatisms, post-ictal anomic aphasia). Occasional right anterior temporal interictal epileptiform discharges confirmed. Findings confirm right temporal lobe epilepsy. The interictal and ictal data are concordant and lateralize to the right temporal lobe.
output.limitations: Only one clinical event captured despite 96 hours of recording. Patient's habitual event frequency (twice monthly) limits capture probability.
recommended_actions: High-resolution 3T MRI with epilepsy protocol for re-evaluation of right hippocampus given now-confirmed right temporal epilepsy
Consider FDG-PET for additional lateralizing and localizing data
Initiate antiepileptic drug therapy for focal epilepsy

findings.location: Right hippocampus, predominantly the body segment
findings.size: Right hippocampal volume reduced; automated volumetry confirms 2.6 cm3 (7th percentile) versus left 3.4 cm3 (42nd percentile)

signal_characteristics.T1_3D: Subtle loss of internal architecture of the right hippocampal body compared to left, now more apparent on high-resolution thin coronal sections (1.5 mm slices)
signal_characteristics.T2_coronal: Mild but definite hyperintense signal in the right hippocampal body relative to the left on thin coronal cuts perpendicular to the long axis of the hippocampus
signal_characteristics.FLAIR_coronal: Subtle FLAIR hyperintensity in the right hippocampal body, confirmed by two independent neuroradiology readers

findings.mass_effect: None. Borderline prominence of the right temporal horn compared to left.
findings.borders: No discrete mass lesion. Changes consistent with early hippocampal sclerosis.

volumetrics.right_hippocampus: 2.6 cm3 (7th percentile for age and sex)
volumetrics.left_hippocampus: 3.4 cm3 (42nd percentile for age and sex)

additional_observations: 3T MRI with dedicated epilepsy protocol including 1.5 mm coronal FLAIR and T2 sequences perpendicular to the hippocampal long axis
Automated hippocampal segmentation performed with FreeSurfer-based pipeline
Periventricular white matter punctate foci unchanged, nonspecific
No focal cortical dysplasia or other dual pathology identified
output.impression: High-resolution 3T epilepsy protocol MRI now demonstrates definite right hippocampal asymmetry with automated volumetric confirmation (asymmetry index 0.13, above the 0.10 threshold). Subtle T2/FLAIR signal increase in the right hippocampal body is confirmed by dual reader review. Findings are consistent with mild right mesial temporal sclerosis. These imaging findings are concordant with right temporal epileptiform activity and ictal onset demonstrated on video-EEG monitoring.
recommended_actions: FDG-PET for concordance if surgical evaluation is considered
Neuropsychological testing for lateralization of memory function

Neuropsychological_Testing.clinical_significance: Below average visual memory, may lateralize to right temporal lobe dysfunction
Impaired visual memory consolidation, consistent with right mesial temporal lobe dysfunction
Average verbal learning, suggesting relatively preserved left temporal function

Neuropsychological_Testing.test: Verbal Memory (CVLT-II Long Delay Free Recall)

Neuropsychological_Testing.clinical_significance: Below average facial recognition, consistent with right temporal dysfunction

output.interpretation: Neuropsychological profile demonstrates a material-specific visual memory deficit with relative preservation of verbal memory. The pattern of impaired visual learning and delayed recall alongside reduced facial recognition is consistent with right temporal lobe dysfunction and lateralizes to the right hemisphere. Verbal memory and naming are preserved, indicating intact left temporal function. Overall cognitive functioning is in the average range. This neuropsychological pattern is concordant with right mesial temporal sclerosis on MRI and right temporal epileptiform activity on EEG.
abnormal_values_summary: Visual learning (BVMT-R Total Learning) T-score 36 - below average, lateralizing to right temporal dysfunction
Visual memory consolidation (BVMT-R Delayed Recall) T-score 33 - impaired, consistent with right hippocampal pathology
Facial recognition (Benton) T-score 37 - below average, supports right temporal lateralization

findings.location: Right mesial and anterolateral temporal lobe

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Reduced FDG uptake in the right mesial temporal structures (hippocampus and parahippocampal gyrus) and mild reduction in the right anterolateral temporal neocortex compared to symmetric uptake on the left
signal_characteristics.asymmetry_index: Right-to-left temporal lobe asymmetry index 0.82 (normal >0.90), indicating moderate right temporal hypometabolism

findings.location: Bilateral frontal, parietal, and occipital lobes; left temporal lobe; basal ganglia; thalami; cerebellum

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Symmetric and normal FDG uptake in remaining brain regions

additional_observations: Interictal FDG-PET acquired during confirmed interictal state (scalp EEG monitored during uptake period)
No hypermetabolic foci to suggest ictal activity during tracer uptake
output.impression: Interictal FDG-PET demonstrates moderate right mesial and anterolateral temporal hypometabolism. This is concordant with right mesial temporal sclerosis on MRI and right temporal epileptiform activity on EEG. Multi-modal concordance supports right temporal lobe epilepsy and is relevant for surgical candidacy evaluation.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Right temporal lobe epilepsy with mesial temporal sclerosis
differential_by_imaging.likelihood: High - concordant across MRI, EEG, and PET

recommended_actions: Present at multi-disciplinary epilepsy surgery conference
Consider Wada test or fMRI for language lateralization
Discuss surgical options with patient if seizures prove drug-resistant

Antiepileptic_Drug_Levels.clinical_significance: Patient was not on any antiepileptic medications at the time of admission. This represents a new diagnosis of focal epilepsy. AED therapy should be initiated.

output.interpretation: No antiepileptic drug levels available as the patient has not been started on AED therapy. The recurrent focal seizures with impaired awareness confirmed on video-EEG monitoring constitute an indication for initiation of antiepileptic drug therapy. Recommended first-line agents for focal epilepsy include levetiracetam, lamotrigine, or lacosamide.

output.query: subtle mesial temporal sclerosis MRI-negative epilepsy quantitative volumetry

results.title: Quantitative MRI in temporal lobe epilepsy: relationship with neuropathology and surgical outcome
results.summary: Automated hippocampal volumetry detected subtle hippocampal atrophy in 30% of visually MRI-negative temporal lobe epilepsy patients, correlating with histopathological hippocampal sclerosis and favorable surgical outcomes.

results.title: Subtle hippocampal malformations and volumetric reduction in temporal lobe epilepsy
results.summary: Quantitative volumetric analysis can reveal hippocampal sclerosis not visible on standard visual MRI interpretation, supporting the use of automated tools in epilepsy presurgical evaluation.

results.title: FDG-PET in MRI-negative temporal lobe epilepsy: lateralizing value and surgical outcomes
results.summary: FDG-PET demonstrated temporal hypometabolism in 70-80% of MRI-negative temporal lobe epilepsy patients, with good surgical outcomes when PET and EEG were concordant.

output.summary: In patients with temporal lobe epilepsy and subtle or initially negative MRI findings, quantitative hippocampal volumetry and FDG-PET provide important additional lateralizing information. Automated volumetric analysis can detect hippocampal sclerosis missed on visual inspection in up to 30% of cases. FDG-PET lateralizes the epileptogenic zone in 70-80% of MRI-negative cases. Multi-modal concordance (EEG, MRI volumetrics, PET, neuropsychology) is essential for surgical planning and predicts favorable postoperative seizure outcomes.

output.proposed: Lamotrigine 25 mg daily, titrate over 6 weeks to 200 mg daily
interactions: No significant interaction with vitamin D3
warnings: Risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome / toxic epidermal necrolysis, especially with rapid titration. Must titrate slowly over 6-8 weeks.
Risk of serious rash is increased with concurrent valproate use (not applicable in this patient)
Patient has penicillin allergy (urticaria) - counsel to report any rash immediately and distinguish from drug hypersensitivity
May require dose adjustment if oral contraceptives are added or removed due to estrogen-glucuronidation interaction
output.formulary_status: On formulary, no prior authorization required
alternatives: Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily, titrate to 1000-1500 mg twice daily (faster titration, but higher risk of psychiatric side effects given patient's anxiety history)
Lacosamide 50 mg twice daily, titrate to 200 mg twice daily
Oxcarbazepine 300 mg twice daily, titrate to 600 mg twice daily (monitor sodium levels)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Focal epilepsy - temporal lobe (right mesial temporal lobe epilepsy with subtle hippocampal sclerosis)

differential.key_features: Episodes of altered awareness with automatisms could mimic PNES. However, video-EEG captured a habitual event with clear right temporal electrographic onset, definitively establishing an epileptic etiology. Stereotyped semiology and post-ictal confusion also support epilepsy.

Episodes occur without postural trigger or prodromal lightheadedness. Prolonged post-ictal confusion (5-7 minutes) and oral/manual automatisms are inconsistent with syncope. Normal ECG argues against cardiac syncope.

Patient is 41 with no significant vascular risk factors. TIAs produce negative symptoms (weakness, numbness) rather than positive phenomena with automatisms. Stereotyped, recurrent nature of episodes is more consistent with epilepsy.

Mother has migraines, but patient's episodes lack the slow spread of aura symptoms typical of migraine (>5 minutes). No headache follows episodes. Duration and semiology (automatisms, post-ictal confusion) are more consistent with temporal lobe seizures.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history from patient and witness including episode semiology, duration, frequency, post-ictal features, and any possible aura symptoms
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Recurrent episodes of altered awareness with oral and manual automatisms, post-ictal confusion, and possible subtle deja vu aura. Stereotyped semiology consistent with temporal lobe seizures.
Normal interictal neurological exam. Subtle memory findings on bedside testing may warrant formal neuropsychological evaluation.
Equivocal right temporal sharp transients and intermittent right temporal slowing - suspicious but not definitive

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI brain with attention to temporal lobes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Subtle right hippocampal asymmetry with borderline volumetric findings, not clearly diagnostic

optimal_actions.action: Order basic labs including BMP, CBC, and urine drug screen to exclude metabolic or toxic causes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal metabolic panel, no toxic or metabolic cause identified

optimal_actions.action: Given equivocal routine EEG findings, arrange prolonged video-EEG monitoring to capture a habitual event
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Captured habitual seizure with right anterior temporal onset, confirming right temporal lobe epilepsy

optimal_actions.action: Request high-resolution 3T MRI with dedicated epilepsy protocol given confirmed right temporal epilepsy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild right mesial temporal sclerosis confirmed with automated volumetry (asymmetry index 0.13)

optimal_actions.action: Initiate antiepileptic drug therapy appropriate for focal epilepsy, considering patient's anxiety history
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Lamotrigine preferred given anxiety comorbidity; levetiracetam is an alternative but carries higher psychiatric side effect risk

optimal_actions.action: Counsel patient on driving restrictions and seizure safety precautions
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient informed of driving restrictions per state law and safety precautions

optimal_actions.action: Arrange neuropsychological testing to characterize cognitive profile and lateralize dysfunction
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Material-specific visual memory deficit lateralizing to right temporal lobe

optimal_actions.action: Consider FDG-PET if pursuing surgical evaluation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Right temporal hypometabolism concordant with MRI and EEG

critical_actions: Obtain EEG to evaluate for epileptiform activity; pursue prolonged video-EEG monitoring if routine EEG is equivocal
Obtain brain MRI to evaluate for structural etiology; pursue high-resolution epilepsy protocol MRI if initial study is equivocal
Obtain basic metabolic panel and CBC to exclude metabolic seizure causes
Initiate antiepileptic drug therapy for focal epilepsy once diagnosis is confirmed
Counsel patient on driving restrictions given recurrent seizures with impaired awareness
Recognize that equivocal initial EEG and MRI findings do not exclude temporal lobe epilepsy and require further investigation
contraindicated_actions: Dismissing episodes as anxiety or panic attacks without obtaining EEG and further workup
Performing lumbar puncture in this clinical context (no signs of infection, inflammation, or elevated intracranial pressure)
Prescribing bupropion which lowers seizure threshold, particularly concerning in a patient with confirmed epilepsy
Starting carbamazepine or phenytoin without careful consideration of side effects and drug interactions in a woman of childbearing age
Allowing continued driving without seizure freedom per state regulations
Discharging without arranging follow-up after equivocal initial workup
key_reasoning_points: Recurrent stereotyped episodes of altered awareness with oral and manual automatisms are highly suggestive of focal seizures with impaired awareness, most commonly originating from the temporal lobe
The subtle and possibly unrecognized deja vu episodes represent possible focal aware seizures (auras) that the patient did not recognize as abnormal
Initial equivocal EEG findings do not exclude epilepsy; a single routine EEG has approximately 50% sensitivity for detecting interictal epileptiform discharges in patients with epilepsy
Subtle MRI findings in temporal lobe epilepsy require dedicated epilepsy protocol imaging with thin coronal cuts and quantitative hippocampal volumetry for detection
The brother's history of a single unprovoked seizure raises the possibility of a genetic predisposition to seizure disorders in this family

Post-ictal anomic aphasia (inability to name objects) after a right temporal seizure may occur due to post-ictal dysfunction spreading to language networks, and does not necessarily indicate left temporal onset
Choice of AED should account for psychiatric comorbidities; lamotrigine may be preferred over levetiracetam in patients with anxiety given the latter's higher rate of psychiatric adverse effects
Multi-modal concordance across EEG, MRI, PET, and neuropsychology is essential for confident lateralization and potential surgical planning in cases with subtle imaging findings
